title: How to Build a Dividend Portfolio from Scratch
description: A single overlooked metric exposes 90% of 'high yield' stocks as complete value traps. Learn the exact step-by-step framework to build an income portfolio that actually survives a recession.

mainEntity.name: How much money do I need to start a dividend portfolio?

acceptedAnswer.text: You can start a dividend portfolio with as little as $100 using fractional shares. The key is consistency. Thanks to commission-free trading, you can slowly build positions in high-quality dividend-paying stocks or ETFs over time.

mainEntity.name: Are dividend stocks safer than growth stocks?

acceptedAnswer.text: Generally, yes. Dividend-paying companies are usually mature, profitable businesses with stable cash flows. While their stock prices still fluctuate, the steady dividend payments provide a cushion during market downturns that pure growth stocks lack.

Most U.S. companies pay dividends on a quarterly basis (four times a year). However, some companies and many Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) and Bond ETFs pay monthly dividends. A smaller number of international companies pay semi-annually or annually.

mainEntity.name: What is a good dividend yield?

acceptedAnswer.text: A 'good' dividend yield typically falls between 2% and 5%. Yields above 6-7% should be viewed with caution, as they often indicate a distressed company whose stock price has plummeted, signaling a potential upcoming dividend cut (a 'dividend trap').

mainEntity.name: Do I have to pay taxes on dividends?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, unless the stocks are held in a tax-advantaged account like a Roth IRA. In a taxable brokerage account, 'qualified' dividends are taxed at the lower long-term capital gains rate (0%, 15%, or 20%), while 'ordinary' dividends (like those from REITs) are taxed at your regular income tax rate.
